model_narrative: We plan a single round trip that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting depot. For each ordered pair of cities we decide whether the trip travels directly from one to the other, and we track the order in which the cities are visited along the route. Traveling directly between two cities incurs a known cost that depends on the pair. The objective is to choose a tour that makes the total travel cost as small as possible.

sets.name: ii
sets.doc: the cities that must all be visited on a single round trip; the first city in the set is the depot where the tour starts and ends

params.name: c
params.doc: the travel cost of going directly from the first city to the second city of an ordered pair; the cost of a leg from a city to itself is not used

params.name: n
params.doc: the total number of cities, a single integer equal to the size of the city set

vars.name: x
vars.doc: arc selection indicator over an ordered pair of cities; equals 1 if the tour travels directly from the first city to the second city and 0 otherwise; the diagonal entries from a city to itself are fixed to 0

vars.name: u
vars.doc: the position of each city along the tour, used for subtour elimination; the depot is pinned to position 0 and every other city takes a position between 0 and the number of cities minus one

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be departed exactly once. For each city, the trip leaves it to go directly to exactly one other city.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be entered exactly once. For each city, the trip arrives at it directly from exactly one other city.

constraints.name: ii)[0]
constraints.intent: The route must form one continuous tour through all the cities rather than splitting into separate disconnected loops. This is enforced by keeping the visiting positions of the cities consistent with the direction of travel, so that whenever the trip goes directly from one city to another, the second city comes later in the visiting order than the first. The starting depot is left out of this requirement, and so is the case of a city compared with itself.

query: Dispatch flagged that the plan leans almost entirely on our short neighborhood hops — the legs that run five units or under. There's a proposal on the table to lean less on those and use at most five of the cheap short legs across the whole loop. Plug that cap in and tell me where the total mileage cost lands.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_cheap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.ii for j in model.ii if i != j and value(model.c[i, j]) <= 5) <= 5)

query: There's talk of closing the city 8 / city 9 connector in both directions for resurfacing. If neither the 8-to-9 nor the 9-to-8 leg can be used, where does the tour cost land?

query: Compliance is reviewing how much the route relies on those free zero-cost connector legs between paired depots. The board wants to test capping that reliance — no more than a fifth of all the legs the driver runs should be the free connectors. Run that scenario and tell me what total cost the optimizer reports.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_free_share = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.ii for j in model.ii if i != j and value(model.c[i, j]) == 0) <= 0.2 * sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.ii for j in model.ii if i != j))

query: Operations is considering taking the city 6 / city 7 corridor offline both ways for a road closure. With neither the 6-to-7 nor the 7-to-6 leg available, what total cost does the routing come out to?

query: City 3 sits way out on its own, and the plan tiptoes in on the cheap city-10 hop and sneaks back out the same cheap corner. Shouldn't the driver commit and come at city 3 head-on from the far cluster of stops out there — at least one of the legs in from city 4, 5, 6, or 7? If we make the route enter city 3 from that group, what's the model seeing that makes it dodge those legs?

query: Looking at the route, every city seems to get reached on one of those free zero-cost connector legs — the plan keeps arriving for nothing. That feels like the model gaming the freebies instead of routing honestly. Couldn't we just require every city to be arrived at on a leg that actually costs something, so no stop is entered via a free connector? What's the tradeoff the model is hiding by leaning on those free arrivals?

query: The route leans hard on that free city 4 / city 5 hop to bounce between those two stops. That feels like the model gaming a zero-cost shortcut rather than planning a sensible loop. If we ban hopping straight between city 4 and city 5 in either direction, what's the real tradeoff the model is hiding?
